title: Church Government, Part 6: Biblical Qualifications for Elders
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro

outline.title: 1. Review of Previous Lessons
subpoints: Christ is the head of the church; elders, shepherds, and bishops refer to the same office
Four wrong forms of church government and the one biblical form
Elders both rule and labor in word and doctrine
The nature of submission to elders in the local church

outline.title: 2. Biblical Qualifications for Elders (1 Timothy 3 and Titus 1)
subpoints: Calvin: these qualities must be present before ordination, not attained afterward
Marshall and Payne's three C-categories: Conviction, Character, and Competency
Character qualities form the majority -- godliness, not skill, is the primary requirement
Competency: the elder must be able to exhort and convict with sound doctrine

outline.title: 3. Samuel Miller's Seven Elder Qualifications
subpoints: Unfamed (authentic) piety and good sense and sound judgment
Sound in the faith and well-informed in gospel truth
Eminent prudence and a good report within and without the church
A man of public spirit, large views, and ardent, importunate (persistent) prayer

outline.title: 4. The Elder Must Be a Man
subpoints: 'Husband of one wife' presupposes maleness; not a requirement to be married
1 Timothy 2:12 prohibits women from teaching men or exercising authority over men
This prohibition is grounded in creation order (Adam formed first), not culture
Teaching and exercising authority are the inherent functions of the elder's office

outline.title: 5. The Elder Must Be Chosen by the People
subpoints: Historical testimony: Calvin, Bucer, and Witherow all affirm congregational election
Matthias chosen by the congregation of 120 brethren (Acts 1)
The seven deacons selected by the congregation and confirmed by the apostles (Acts 6)
Acts 14:23 -- 'appointed' by cheirotonia (showing of hands), confirmed by apostolic authority
The Holy Spirit uses the congregation as his instrument in appointing elders

outline.title: 6. The Elder Must Rule Well
subpoints: 1 Timothy 5:17: double honor to elders who rule well
Ruling one's own household well is the proving ground for ruling the church
Elders will give an account to God for how they shepherded the flock (Hebrews 13:17)
Homework: read Ezekiel 34 and identify the faithful and faithless shepherd

summary:
This Sunday School lesson is the sixth installment in the Church Government series. Pastor Tito Lyro leads the class through a comprehensive review of the biblical qualifications required for the office of elder, drawing primarily from 1 Timothy 3:1-7 and Titus 1:5-9. He uses Colin Marshall and Tony Payne's framework from The Trellis and the Vine to organize the qualifications into three C-categories -- Conviction, Character, and Competency -- emphasizing that the vast majority of the requirements are moral rather than skill-based, and that they describe the floor of Christian manhood, not a ceiling reserved only for special leaders.

The lesson addresses who may serve as elder, explaining from 1 Timothy 2:12 and the creation-order argument that only men are qualified. He also surveys 19th-century Princeton theologian Samuel Miller, who distilled the requirements into seven characteristics including authentic piety, sound judgment, doctrinal soundness, prudence, good reputation, broad vision, and persistent prayer.

A significant portion of the class explores the congregational basis for elder election. Citing Calvin, Bucer, and Thomas Witherow, Pastor Lyro argues that officers of the church must be chosen by the people. He walks through Acts 1 (election of Matthias), Acts 6 (the seven deacons), and Acts 14:23 (appointment by showing of hands), demonstrating that the biblical pattern is the Holy Spirit working through the congregation's vote.

The lesson closes with the qualification that an elder must rule well, noting that how a man governs his family is the proving ground for how he will govern the church. An assignment is given to read Ezekiel 34 and identify what faithful and faithless shepherding looks like.
transcript:
## Church Government, Part 6: Biblical Qualifications for Elders

*Pastor Tito Lyro -- Sunday School*

---

Great to see you guys again. We're continuing our series on Church Government. Before we start a new lesson, I wanted to hear from you where we have been. This is our sixth lesson. What have we seen?

We're learning about authority and submission. All the wrong ways of Church Government -- four wrong ways and one right way. The way the term "elders" is used throughout the Scriptures in both Old and New Testaments. Christ is the head of the Church. There are different roles for elders -- all rule, some also labor in Word and doctrine. We talked about what submission is. And we looked at elders, shepherds, and bishops/overseers -- and what we learned is they're all referring to the same office in the Bible.

**Today we're going to consider the fact that elders must be biblically qualified.** Not everybody is supposed to be an elder. The Bible itself tells us what the qualifications are for a person to be an elder. There are two passages that explicitly list these qualifications.

### 1 Timothy 3:1-7

> *This is a faithful saying: If a man desires the position of a bishop, he desires a good work. A bishop then must be blameless, the husband of one wife, temperate, sober-minded, of good behavior, hospitable, able to teach, not given to wine, not violent, not greedy for money, but gentle, not quarrelsome, not covetous; one who rules his own house well, having his children in submission with all reverence (for if a man does not know how to rule his own house, how will he take care of the church of God?); not a novice, lest being puffed up with pride he fall into the same condemnation as the devil. Moreover, he must have a good testimony among those who are outside, lest he fall into reproach and the snare of the devil.*

### Titus 1:5-9

> *For this reason I left you in Crete, that you should set in order the things that are lacking, and appoint elders in every city as I commanded you -- if a man is blameless, the husband of one wife, having faithful children not accused of dissipation or insubordination. For a bishop must be blameless, as a steward of God, not self-willed, not quick-tempered, not given to wine, not violent, not greedy for money, but hospitable, a lover of what is good, sober-minded, just, holy, self-controlled, holding fast the faithful word as he has been taught, that he may be able, by sound doctrine, both to exhort and convict those who contradict.*

### Three Categories of Qualifications

John Calvin suggests these qualities are to be present *prior* to ordination. Colin Marshall and Tony Payne, in *The Trellis and the Vine*, group the qualifications into three categories starting with the letter C:

**Conviction qualities:** Not a novice; must hold fast to the faithful word.

**Character qualities:** Most of the qualifications are character, not skills. Blameless, husband of one wife, temperate, sober-minded, of good behavior, hospitable, not given to wine, not violent, not greedy, gentle, not quarrelsome, not covetous. These are interconnected: temperate connects to sober-minded, which connects to not given to much wine, which connects to not violent, which connects to being gentle. These are *floor* qualities -- what every Christian man in the church should exhibit, not a ceiling to aspire toward.

**Competency qualities:** Must be able to speak God's word profitably -- rules his house well, exhorts and convicts those who contradict.

### Samuel Miller's Seven Qualifications

Samuel Miller spent about 50 years studying eldership in the 19th century and is the best voice in American Presbyterianism on this subject. He says an elder must have:

1. Unfamed (authentic) piety
2. Good sense and sound judgment
3. Soundness in the faith and gospel truth
4. Eminent prudence
5. A good report within and without the church
6. Public spirit and large views
7. Ardent zeal and importunate (persistent) prayer

### The Elder Must Be a Man

Maleness is a qualification. The Bible says in 1 Timothy 3:2 the overseer must be the husband of one wife -- this does not require every elder to be married, but it does require that if married, he is the husband of only one woman. Only men can fulfill that.

Also 1 Timothy 2:12 says: *"I do not permit a woman to teach or to have authority over a man."* The basis in verse 13 is creation order -- "For Adam was formed first, then Eve." This is not cultural but creational. Teaching and exercising authority are inherent functions of the elder's office; therefore women are not to be elders.

### The Elder Must Be Chosen by the People

Thomas Witherow calls this the first apostolic principle of church government. Calvin says men are to be selected by a vote of the congregation. Bucer says election should be according to the godly consensus and will of the people.

Biblically:
- **Acts 1:15-26:** Matthias was selected by the 120 brethren gathered together. The congregation chose two men; the apostles confirmed one through lots.
- **Acts 6:1-6:** The apostles told the congregation to "seek out from among you seven men" meeting the qualifications. The congregation chose them; the apostles confirmed and ordained them by laying on hands.
- **Acts 14:23:** Paul and Barnabas "appointed" (cheirotonia -- by showing of hands) elders -- that is, they confirmed the people's choice.

So the elder is divinely appointed by the Holy Spirit through the mediation of the congregational election. Acts 20:28: *"The Holy Spirit has made you overseers."* The Holy Spirit uses you, the congregation, to make sure that you have shepherds that glorify God.

### The Elder Must Rule Well

1 Timothy 5:17: *"Let the elders who rule well be counted worthy of double honor."* Paul knows there will sometimes be elders who do not rule well. He says honor those who do. The elder rules his private life well, and he rules the church well.

Hebrews 13:17: *"Obey those who rule over you, and be submissive, for they watch out for your souls, as those who must give account."* All elders will give an account to the Lord for how they ruled.

**Homework:** Read Ezekiel 34 with this in mind -- the first ten verses describe the faithless shepherds (avoid what they do), then the faithful shepherds (imitate what they do), then Christ the shepherd (learn from Him). That's how the passage is structured.
